summary: Joe Reis criticizes the flood of pundits who claimed to predict the OpenAI leadership drama, arguing that genuine uncertainty should be met with humility rather than false confidence. He reflects on the nature of forecasting, LLM limitations in data management, and the realities of startup life versus the dream of building businesses.
key_points: Pundits claiming to predict complex events like the OpenAI debacle are mostly charlatans chasing engagement
LLMs have both a 'science problem' (consistency/correctness) and a 'product problem' (answers vs. achieving aims)
The startup/founder life is often a twilight existence of being 'tenuously wealthy on paper' with no clear exit
Specialized industry clouds often fail because engineering teams prefer building custom competitive advantages
money_quotes: If you predicted the exact outcome of the events of OpenAI last week, please make this public. Could you prove you knew every tiny twist and turn as it happened, second by second? Otherwise (at least to me), you're a charlatan and bullshit artist.
'I don't know' are often the best three words you can say in situations of major uncertainty. Because you often don't know. Simple as that.
It's like dealing with an employee (at best, an intern) who does mundane work and oscillates between decent and poor performance on an hourly basis.
I'd rather focus on running an actual business that does weird things like serving customers who pay money and making a profit.
key_insight: Intellectual humility and admitting uncertainty is far more valuable than the false confidence of pundits who chase engagement by pretending to predict unpredictable events.
